chunk_summary: Guidelines for fluid-restricted feeding in pediatric patients with congenital heart disease (CHD) and chronic lung disease, focusing on energy-dense nutrition, aspiration risk, and feeding strategies to optimize growth and minimize complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the challenges of fluid-restricted feeding in pediatric CHD and chronic lung disease.
Learn strategies to deliver adequate calories and protein within fluid limits using energy-dense formulas.
Recognize the importance of monitoring growth and reassessing feeding plans every 1–2 weeks.
Identify risks of overfeeding and strategies to minimize aspiration and respiratory compromise.
key_facts: Energy-dense formulas or modular strategies are preferred to meet caloric needs within fluid restrictions.
Excess carbohydrate intake can increase CO2 production and worsen ventilator dependence; calories should not exceed measured or estimated resting energy expenditure (REE) during acute illness.
Continuous or slow gravity feeding is better tolerated than large boluses, especially in high-risk patients.
Persistent vomiting should prompt reassessment of tube position, constipation, and delivery rate before changing formulas.
Early transition to gastrostomy is recommended if inadequate intake is expected for more than several weeks, provided caregiver capacity and supply access are secure.
reference_quote: The practical goal is to deliver adequate calories and protein in a smaller volume while minimizing emesis and respiratory compromise.
document_key_topics: Fluid-restricted feeding in pediatric CHD and chronic lung disease
Monitoring growth and reassessing feeding plans
